# Text-to-SQL and Natural Language Interfaces to Databases

Natural language interfaces (NLI) to databases aim to translate user queries expressed in natural language into executable database queries, enabling non-expert users to access structured data without requiring knowledge of query syntax. Text-to-SQL is the most extensively studied variant of this problem, with a rich history of approaches spanning multiple paradigms.

**Sketch-based methods** represent one of the earliest approaches to structured query synthesis. These methods map user queries to SQL by first predicting a high-level SQL pattern—a sketch—and then performing slot filling to generate the complete query. SQLNet and TypeSQL are representative examples of this paradigm, which decomposes the generation problem into template selection and value prediction stages.

**Sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) methods** treat text-to-SQL as a translation problem analogous to machine translation. Seq2SQL introduced a seq2seq model augmented with reinforcement learning for SQL generation from natural language, establishing a family of encoder-decoder approaches that map input utterances directly to output query strings.

**Tree- and structure-based methods** construct SQL queries by assembling semantically meaningful subtrees or leveraging the inherent hierarchical structure of SQL. RAT-SQL introduces a relation-aware transformer that models the question and database schema as a relational graph, enabling effective schema linking—the alignment of natural language mentions with schema elements. SmBoP employs a chart-based bottom-up decoder to generate SQL as compositional trees, building queries incrementally from smaller fragments. SyntaxSQLNet uses a syntax tree-based decoder that leverages SQL grammar rules to guide generation, belonging to the broader family of grammar-based or syntax-constrained decoding methods. These approaches ensure syntactic correctness by construction, using formal production rules to constrain the decoder's output space so that only well-formed queries can be produced.

These text-to-SQL techniques have influenced approaches to other structured query languages, though direct transfer to graph query languages like Cypher faces significant challenges. Differences in underlying data models (relational tables versus labeled property graphs), traversal patterns (joins versus path expressions), and schema structures (rigid relational schemas versus flexible graph schemas) mean that methods optimized for SQL generation require substantial adaptation to handle graph-native query constructs effectively.

## See Also

- [[z20260318-007|Labeled Property Graphs, Graph Query Languages, and Multi-Agent Query Generation]] — extends text-to-SQL paradigms to the graph domain, detailing how text-to-Cypher generation over property graphs inherits challenges from and builds upon the structured query synthesis methods developed for relational databases, while introducing additional complexities from graph-specific schema variability, multi-hop traversal, and entity hallucination
- [[z20260318-006|Retrieval-Augmented Generation and GraphRAG]] — grounds the connection between structured query generation and retrieval-augmented pipelines, where text-to-SQL and text-to-Cypher serve as downstream mechanisms enabling the query execution component of graph-guided retrieval stages
